speaker: interviewer
text: --6. Fira, you went to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Krakow, we been-- it might be three weeks because we didn't have the conditions, of living conditions. So came a friend from, from my husband, and he asked us to come to live in Waldenburg. There they had, they used to throw out the German. Because a lot of Germans were actually-- Waldenburg was taken from the Germans by the Polish government. So they, a lot of Germans, went to Germany. And it was left flats and places to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this friend came. And he asked us to go over. So we stayed there and-- [SIGHS] Did you go back to Chelm at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. This time, I left my sister, my brother-in-law there. This wagon, we didn't know where it goes. It went to Cieszyn. And then I-- they, they knew the address of Moniek's sisters. So we got the letter where they are. And then we were in, in Wal-- in Wal-- we were, I don't know, might be six weeks. And we left after the Kielce pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were registered. It was a big [NON-ENGLISH] like, you know they'd been-- how do you say-- advertising the Jewish agency to leave Poland. So we were registered, and we left with our organization. It was our, the like [NON-ENGLISH] soldiers because Moniek was in Polish army during the war. And I, I was the only one with, with a baby. Anyway we came, we left. It was a Saturday, I think. And we crossed the border, the Czechoslovakian border. It was so well-organized. It was waiting little trains. And then we came to-- this brought us to Bratislava. And Bratislava was already Jewish committee there. And they fed children and made lunches for us, sandwiches and all that. And they put us in good trains. And from there, they told us that we are traveling. If somebody comes and asks, we are traveling as Greek, not to speak [BACKROUND NOISE] Polish, not to speak Yiddish. And they ask for a lot of documents. Lots of people-- a lot of people gave away. And from there, we came to, to Vienna. [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Vienna, in Vienna, we stayed only one, one night. When we came in the [INAUDIBLE] house, which was, you know, for like a, a, a passing, when you came in there, on the walls up to the ceiling was names, people looking for each other, names and addresses and they left. And it, it was-- the whole walls were written down. Anyway, from there, they put us, again, in wagons. And we-- they were bringing us to our camp. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that, that camp was near Nuremberg. And the day we, we arrived to Nuremberg, that was the, the-- how you say, the finish of the war. How you say in English? Say it in Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: The [POLISH]. You know? That finished the, the war criminal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the trials.

speaker: interviewee
text: The trials. It was the date the trial was finished. From there, they brought us-- I don't know what it is. It must be like, like [INAUDIBLE] here. So the, the name was Kamp, kamp. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-M-P, kamp. And that was the aerodrome. And on this aerodrome, it was might be 10,000 people. It was made like streets from tents. And we lived in, in these tents. And it was already cold, start to be autumn and people made-- that was the American. We were already on the American zone. And we-- they, they made the hunger strike that we are here. , [? And we-- ?] they should think where to put us. We are displaced people. And so it came out a big commission. And they had speeches in, in English and translated. Anyway, they told us that they are clearing up because it was a lot of Polish people in camps. And they say that they have a country. They can go home, or they can go live separately in even Germany. And so they are cleaning-- clearing out these people. And we will be given a, a, a place to live. It just happened that they brought us to Wetzlar near Frankfurt am Main. And it was five, 10 blocks, three, three-story blocks that was the concern. It was a long corridor with rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to pack in the bigger rooms, two or three families. And because I was already with my brother, and so we had a small little room. Later came another transfer, so they put me a strange man there. And-- but it was already under our roof. [LAUGHS] And there, we-- we got food from UNRRA. And we lived there for three years. Did you work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't any work. No. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you see the future in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we, we saw people-- a lot of people were doing black market, you know, and they somehow, they lived. Moniek used to go in the country to bring some chickens and eggs and sell to, to the people. That's how we survived. And the rest we had from, from the UNRRA.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made you decide to leave? How did that-- how did you come to that decision?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Moniek's sister, her husband's brother was in Australia from Vienna before the war. They came here before the war. So they sent permit to them. She was still in Poland. And so she came here. And she, she was only about two years in Australia. And then she started-- she sent us a permit. And my-- so I parted with my brother. He went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewee
text: There, being in the camp, which was the liberation of, of the Jewish state. That, that's a evening-- that's a day which will be in my memory forever. It was a Sunday. And we, we didn't know. You know, it wasn't any news-- newspapers, no radios. You, you, you didn't know. And we were-- suddenly arrived a Jeep and General Clark. He was the commander over the-- this part occupied Germany army. He-- [INAUDIBLE] we had like a football field. So he arrived on this. And people start to run and to look what, what happened. Then they brought up a microphone. And he said that he-- that was the nearest camp we, we were. He was in Frankfurt, so we were the nearest camp. And he came to to, you know, to give us the, the honor that, that proclaimed the Jewish state. In the evening, he stayed on-- that was with his wife on, on the Jeep. In the evening, we had the gate, and it was the whole camp. We were 10,000 people came to the front and came the first Jewish ambassador, such a little man. I don't know who it was at that time. And the gate opens. And a rabbi came with bread and salt to greet him. And when he-- he came by car, but he stopped and walked in. And the American military orchestra played 'Hatikvah.' That was-- and the whole night, people were dancing. And, and, it, it was such a joyful day. That was the first time--

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] So Fira, when did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking how much-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arrived in August-- the 11th of August in 1949 [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] by boat. What was the name of the boat you came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The It was very nice, very good boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of refugees like you? Many refugees on the boat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a few families. Mostly, they went to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot were from Czechoslovakia and Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any non-Jews, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, only Jews. We came from the camp from Wetzlar. We came to Stuttgart. And from Stuttgart, we came to, to Paris. We are-- we were in Paris a month, and then we came to Genoa and board the boat there and here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came, you know, with nothing. My sister-in-law had-- we were living three families in a cottage. I had a little room. And they prepared only a mattress for us and a little wardrobe. And it was two-- how you call this [NON-ENGLISH]? Arm chairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Arm chairs. So-- and we put the two arm chairs together. And she was sleeping [LAUGHS] at night when the arm chairs parted. She, she used to fall down. And the worst was the language, but we found it was a big strike that time when we arrived was very hard with work. Was just start-- started to be a little bit better. It wasn't electricity, was only in the evening for two or three hours and gas. The coal strike was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow, Moniek find some work in, in a factory wherever [INAUDIBLE] they used to make lamps or something. And [SIGHS] I went to work for a Hungarian dressmaker. I, I, I'm very handy, and I'm very particular. And when she asked me if I-- and because I was so neat, she was working a better-- she was one very famous dressmaker in, in Budapest. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Powell. And because she-- people knew her, she had Hungarian better clientele. And my work was so neat, and so I used to get more than wages. [INAUDIBLE] And she-- and I said, I am a dressmaker, and being there, I discovered things which I, I learned she didn't know. And so I earned wages. And Moniek had-- I had six pound. Moniek had nine pound, I think. From this I paid for the-- we lived together, so they divided. I don't remember. Anyway, we decided from this money, I paid for kindergarten and, and how much-- we saved every week 10 pound. And for this 10 pound, after exactly one year, we had enough money. We paid key money for a, for a flat, a one bedroom and lunch room.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did you find the local Australian population?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very, very hostile.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- [SIGHS] it's not like todays really nothing. They're used to it. They mix with other languages, with other food. And and you know, they, they travel a lot. So they know-- they see the world, too. When we came, they, they thought that we are going to take away from them. And when they had a, a, a language, they-- you know, sometimes if it was across line, they use abuse on the telephone. And they-- they couldn't stand it. And also you know, Ann, as a little baby, I traveled with her in the tram or something. And, and when you-- I couldn't speak English, so I spoke to her. They just turned their heads in such a-- you know, they didn't try to help you. And I learned for a year knowing that I am going to Australia. I had a teacher. And one year, he was teaching us English in Germany. But when I came here-- you know, just the basic only. But when I came here, nobody-- he he said we are so good. And nobody could understand us. First of all, he was a German teaching English with a German accent. So to start off, Moniek went to school. I couldn't leave the baby, so I learned all what she learned in kindergarten. She came, that was a big-- every day, I took her to kindergarten. She-- I had to go by-- this bus took me to kindergarten. And I had to leave her there. And the bus turned around. It was a [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was the same bus I have to go not to be late to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So every time the beginning, she used to grab and cry in Polish [SPEAKING POLISH]. Mama, don't leave me here. And every time I went to work, all the way, I was crying. Anyway, she get-- she didn't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: She came home. She said teacher does not understand her. And she was so determined that she start to learn every-- what she learned-- every language, [LAUGHS] every word. She came home. She told me. And I had to repeat. And that's how I learned. And later a lot from-- you know, I start to read English. And still my accent will never-- I miss a lot of words. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Fira, that-- what about the local Jewish population? How did you find your interaction with them, the ones who-- the Australian Jews from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I lived at Randwick. I didn't see any-- I wasn't in contact with any. Then we moved-- after one year, we moved to Bondi. And I met a neighbor there. And he used to ask questions how we survived. And he said, and then you are still looking like this? They thought we would be skeletons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and then he used to complain that we also had this rule. We, we didn't-- during the war, we didn't have butter and such rations. I said, what? Not only butter, I didn't, I didn't even seen butter. You didn't get enough, but I didn't even seen butter. They, they, they couldn't-- I understand. They couldn't imagine. They couldn't imagine. Fira, before we finish the interview, is there anything that you would like to tell me that you've forgotten or an incident that perhaps is important that you haven't mentioned?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think so, only maybe about, about my brother, how I found him. And I parted so many times. In the end was that we parted. He went to Israel. I, I came to, to Australia. Yes. But when were living Taganrog, and we lost each other and then when I found out where he is, he was on [INAUDIBLE]. He was mobilized in a, in a working army. And he was in [NON-ENGLISH] making roads in the mountains. When-- that was when I got the letter from him. And when it came, the repatriation, my-- my sister and I want him also to apply to go back to Poland. He took it-- he took it very hard about, you know, our parents. I was a girl. I was young. I was adventurous. And he, he, he took it even much worser than I did. And when we wrote to him letters to apply, he didn't-- he said he's already-- after the war, he was free. He went to work there, not such a good conditions. But he had up to himself there. And he wrote to-- where will he go. And till the last day, we were already in the wagon. And I went to say goodbye to my neighbor. And that they also-- like a miracle, I, I didn't have to go, but somehow I went back to the place where we lived. And the letter arrived that he applied. So we were very happy that he's going back. And we didn't know where we are going and where he will be. And [SIGHS] we came to [NON-ENGLISH]. And what happened, when he came, when he came back to Poland, he didn't have anybody, not, not a soul.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he, he went to our organization. I, I don't remember, [NON-ENGLISH] or something. I don't know what organization, which were also that I'm-- that, you know, every organization that time was to go to, to Palestine, to Israel. And [SIGHS] so this organizations, they've been on the way like we did, to cross the border to go to, to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went to Krakow. And in Krakow, he went to, to the state [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] baths. And he was in a cubicle. And in the next cubicle, he had talking to men about how the baths was in Stalinsk. and all the-- so when he finished, he came off.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you were in Stalinsk? And yes, and they found out. Well, did-- do you know us and my sister? And they said, yes, that I stepped out in Krakow. They, they knew because it was very seldom somebody got family to take them, you know, to them. And so he went there to, to Moniek's address, to Moniek's sister. And he found out that we are in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And just happened that this-- his organization was passing-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] was closer to the Czechoslovakian border. And they've been passing [INAUDIBLE]. And suddenly, 1:00 in the morning, somebody knocked at the door. And I opened it. It was my brother. Otherwise, we wouldn't have find each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and I asked him what are you doing? I'm so glad you are here. You know, after four, four, five years, we didn't seen each other. He looked. He looked. [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I knew. I took it out, a few shirts from my husband and a warm jumper.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't want. He said, look, with you, you don't know. You are with a child, with a baby. You don't know where-- we are going to, to Israel. We are going to Palestine. They thought they will cross the border and they will go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, I'm with organization. We are group. We keep together, so-- and we didn't know that we will go to Australia. So we will meet him in, in, in Israel. And so he, he left. And when we came back, we were traveling, crossing, you know, the borders, we came-- also, I forgot to say-- to, to Linz. From, I think from Vienna, we went to Linz. And that was a ex-concentration camp, a big-- still was the writing, this [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in. And we had still this barracks and this banks there. And was still the kitchen and, and all, all this. And, and it was another camp. It was two concentration camps. So they brought people which were traveling. A lot of people left Hungary, Czechoslovakia. And when-- so from the other camp, when they found out they brought new people here, they used to come to visit.

speaker: interviewer
text: So came one young man, my brother's age, came to this camp, to our camp. And you know you start to look around from where somebody to look for people. And I recognized him. And he said, you are here? Your brother is there at the other camp. And I didn't know. So anyway, I went to, to see him. And I had to have a, like a, you know, [SPEAKING POLISH]. Pass.

speaker: interviewee
text: A pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: Permission. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, to go from the camp. So I left Moniek with the baby, and I went. I came to the tram to, to travel. You know, I want to enter a tram. And a, a, a German woman kicked me. And I fell, fell, fell over. Anyway, it frightened me. Because I thought anything happen, nobody will know what happened to me. And I came to this camp. It was a huge camp, very big. Here, in this camp where we were, were barracks. There, they had like two-story places. And I went from one to another and asked and for this organization. And when I came, I-- and I asked for my brother. They showed me the room. And it was hanging or drying the jumper I gave him. So I knew he was-- he just was in the pictures, they told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I left a message. I came back. I was so happy. And the next day, he came to visit me. And I started again, come-- the second time, come stay with us. Don't-- we will part again. And who knows, you know? I was already without my sister. And, and, and here, the three of us, we, we everyone separate. And I beg him, and I tried to throw this friend of ours to talk him into to it, to-- eventually, it took maybe a week, he decided to come back.
